channel.title: Political Science, History, and Statistics on Andre Walter
channel.description: Recent content in Political Science, History, and Statistics on Andre Walter

item.title: Talks 2018
item.description: There are a couple of upcoming talks and conferences in 2018 where I’ll present some of my ongoing work. I’m going to cover the following topics at the ECPR im Hamburg, the DVPW in Frankfurt a.M., the ESPAnet in Vilnius and the Political Science Department Seminar in Tübingen: A Socialist Threat? Radical Party Entry, Electoral Competition, and the Formation of Alliances in Majoritarian Systems (Tübingen; ECPR; DVPW) The Social Origins of Christian Democracy.

item.title: CV
item.description: Academic Employment 2018– Postdoctoral Researcher, Chair for Comparative Political Economy and Public Policy, Department of Political Science, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland. 2012–2018 Research Assistant, Chair for Comparative Political Economy and Public Policy, Department of Political Science, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland. 2011–2012 Teaching Assistant, Gender Studies, University of Zurich, Switzerland. 2009–2010 Student and Teaching Assistant, Institute of Sociology, University of Jena, Germany. Education 2018 PhD, Political Science, University of St.

item.title: Publications
item.description: Published (*peer-reviewed) (*)A Race to the Middle. The Politics of Interstate Cost Distribution and Welfare State Expansion. The Journal of Politics, forthcoming (*)Measuring the Impacts of Colonialism: A New Data Set for the Countries of Africa and Asia. Journal of World-Systems Research, 23(1), 2017, 156-190 (with Patrick Ziltener and Daniel Kuenzler) Other Publications (*peer-reviewed) (*)Europäische Integration auf Unternehmensebene: Die Schweizer Beteiligung an Europäischen Betriebsräten (EBR).

item.title: Teaching
item.description: 2018 State and Economy (Multiple Guest Lectures, invited by Prof. Patrick Emmenegger), Lecture for MA Students, University of St. Gallen (Autumn Term) The Politics of Electoral Systems: Political Conflict and Representation, Seminar for MA Students, University of St. Gallen (Spring Term) 2017 Causal Arguments and Analysis: Guest lecture invited by Prof. Patrick Emmenegger, University of St. Gallen (Spring Term) 2013-2016 (Teaching Assistant at the University of St.

item.title: HSG International Fellowship
item.description: I’ve got news that I have been awared with the International Postdoctoral Fellowship! This great opportunity allows me to conduct independent research for up to three years on a number of topics of my choosing at the University of St. Gallen. I planning to focus on two projects: The introduction of proportional representation I haven written a piece with Patrick Emmenegger on the introduction of PR for the election of the Swiss National Council (currently under review).

item.title: Contact
item.description: André Walter Department of Political Science University of St. Gallen Müller-Friedberg-Strasse 6/8 9000 St. Gallen Switzerland eMail: andre[dot]walter[at]unisg[dot]ch

item.title: Research
item.description: I’m interested in a lot of things. For instance, I have published with others on a number of different topics such as QCA or the impact of colonialism. In addition, I have supported research projects with regard to data collection and statistical analysis. However, the main projects I’m working on are the following: The Evolution of Taxation In a project with Patrick Emmenegger and Lucas Leemann, we investigate how the composition of public revenue evolved during the 19th and 20th century and how political institutions shape taxation with focus on income taxation in the Swiss cantons 1830-1980.

item.title: Research
item.description: I’m interested in a lot of things. For instance, I have published with others on a number of different topics such as QCA or the impact of colonialism. In addition, I have supported research projects with regard to data collection and statistical analysis. However, the main projects I’m working on are the following: The Evolution of Taxation In a project with Patrick Emmenegger and Lucas Leemann, we investigate how the composition of public revenue evolved during the 19th and 20th century and how political institutions shape taxation with focus on income taxation in the Swiss cantons 1830-1980.

item.title: About
item.description: I’m a postdoctoral researcher at the Department of Political Science, University of St. Gallen. My areas of interest are comparative political economy, comparative politics and statistical methods. More specifically, I’m interested in the formation and development of parties and electoral institutions as well as social and economic policies in the 19th and early 20th century. In addition, I focus on current topics on comparative political economy. Methodological, I’m interested in Econometrics, GIS and statistical programming in R.

item.title: Swiss Cantonal Governments 1848-2017
item.description: I’m currently working with Patrick Emmenegger on creating a database on the ideological composition of cantonal governments. We were able to compile a time series for most cantons starting in the mid 19th century. A complete Time-Series Cross-Section is available for all cantons for 1894 onwards (except Appenzell Inner-Rhodes which starts 1928 and, of course, Jura in 1978). Below, you can find the first exploratory results. The first plot displays the smoothed government volatility for 24 cantons (excl.

item.title: Estimation of Long Run Effects in R
item.description: A lot of quantitative data in political science comes in the form of “Time-Series Cross-Sectional” (TSCS) datasets. Such datasets usually consist of a low number of cross-sectional units (e.g. states, countries) and higher number of time periods. Since the introduction of panel-corrected standard errors in political science in 1995 by Beck and Katz, political science scholars have discussed the merits and pitfalls of TSCS data. One controversial aspect is the inclusion of lagged dependent variables (LDVs) in estimation equations.
